channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Judge Reopens Trump’s Lawsuit Demanding $10 Billion From IRS
item.description: The ruling was a blow to both President Trump, who had voluntarily dismissed the suit last week, and to the Justice Department, which used the suit to establish a fund likely intended for Trump allies.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer and Andrew Duehren
item.media:description: In a stern order on Friday, a judge said that she wanted to investigate the circumstances surrounding President Trump’s efforts to settle the lawsuit in a way that benefited him and his allies.

item.title: Federal Judge Bars Trump From Immediately Setting Up $1.8 Billion Fund
item.description: A judge in Virginia temporarily blocked the Trump administration from transferring money in or out of the fund until the court could hear arguments in June.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:description: The order came from the Federal District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia, in Alexandria, in a case brought by a group of people who say they have faced partisan attacks by the Trump administration but expect to be excluded from accessing the fund.

item.title: Justice Department Lurches Toward Trump’s Goals
item.description: Plus, the Kennedy Center must remove Trump’s name. Here’s the latest at the end of Friday.
item.dc:creator: Evan Gorelick
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: E. Jean Carroll in 2024 after a hearing on one of her lawsuits.

item.title: Bondi, Pressed Over Epstein Files, Places Responsibility on Blanche and Patel
item.description: Her remarks, delivered during a closed-door interview before the House Oversight Committee, were a candid admission of her own powerlessness.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush and Michael Gold
item.media:description: Former Attorney General Pam Bondi arrives for the House Oversight Committee closed hearing on Friday.

item.title: Actually, Most Immigrants Won’t Need to Leave U.S. to Get Green Cards, D.H.S. Says
item.description: But details remained scant after officials said last week that, with “extraordinary” exceptions, people seeking permanent residency must first leave the country.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz, Madeleine Ngo and Lydia DePillis
item.media:credit: Valerie Plesch for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Department of Homeland Security appeared to make a partial walk-back of a change announced last Friday.

item.title: ICE Agents to Leave Site of Volatile Protests at Detention Center
item.description: Clashes between protesters and armed federal agents have erupted at the parking lot of Delaney Hall in Newark since the Memorial Day weekend.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ley
item.media:description: Federal agents and demonstrators who were protesting conditions inside the Delaney Hall detention center in Newark have faced off for nearly a week.

item.title: Iran’s Hard-Liners Try to Derail Potential Deal With the U.S.
item.description: A political fight is playing out in Iran, where the small but loud faction of hard-liners has used rallies, state media and private and public statements to try to undermine negotiations.
item.dc:creator: Farnaz Fassihi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi/Polaris for The New York Times
item.media:description: A billboard of the late supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, and his successor, Mojtaba Khamenei, during a ceremony in Tehran on Sunday.

item.title: Israeli Troops Push Deeper Into Lebanon as Cease-Fire Talks Continue
item.description: Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said Israeli ground forces had crossed Lebanon’s Litani River, as military officials from the two countries were set to meet for U.S.-brokered talks.
item.dc:creator: Euan Ward
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An apartment building in Tyre, Lebanon, on Tuesday, in the aftermath of an Israeli strike.

item.title: Louisiana Approves Map Eliminating a Majority-Black District
item.description: A Supreme Court court ruling last month rejected the state’s previous congressional map as an illegal racial gerrymander and set off a redistricting race across the South.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane
item.media:credit: Gerald Herbert/Associated Press, via Associated Press Photo, via Gerald Herbert
item.media:description: State Representative Edmond Jordan of Louisiana speaks with fellow lawmakers before a Louisiana House vote on a redistricting plan to eliminate a majority-Black congressional district in response to a U.S. Supreme Court ruling, in Baton Rouge, La., on Thursday.

item.title: How Bad Are President Trump’s Approval Ratings?
item.description: Our chief political analyst, Nate Cohn, looks at President Trump’s sinking approval ratings. How do they compare with polling from other recent presidencies?
item.dc:creator: Nate Cohn, Laura Bult, June Kim, Edward Vega, Jon Miller and Whitney Shefte

item.title: Gretchen Whitmer’s Approach to Trump Was Working With the President
item.description: Ms. Whitmer, the Michigan governor and one of the country’s most prominent Democrats, has been conciliatory with Mr. Trump. As she mulls the future, her party is split on that approach.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gov. Gretchen Whitmer of Michigan stood beside President Donald Trump at Selfridge Air National Guard Base in Michigan last year.

item.title: Trump Endorses Pamela Evette in South Carolina Governor’s Race
item.description: Pamela Evette had been among the most vocal proponents of redrawing the state’s congressional map to help Republicans in the midterms.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs and Emily Cochrane
item.media:description: President Trump on Friday endorsed South Carolina’s lieutenant governor, Pamela Evette, to be the state’s next governor.

item.title: The Politics of the Downwardly Mobile Professional Class
item.description: The debate over whether Graham Platner is “working class” comes at a time when more and more people are at risk of falling into it.
item.dc:creator: Noam Scheiber

item.title: Who Is the Prosecutor Investigating E. Jean Carroll’s Lawsuits?
item.description: Andrew S. Boutros, the Trump-appointed U.S. attorney for the Northern District of Illinois, is said to be overseeing the criminal investigation.
item.dc:creator: Julie Bosman and Danny Hakim
item.media:credit: E. Jason Wambsgans/Chicago Tribune, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Andrew S. Boutros, the U.S. attorney for the Northern District of Illinois, in Chicago in April.

item.title: After China Orders a Times Reporter to Leave the Country, the U.S. Reciprocates
item.description: The Trump administration ejected a Chinese journalist amid fresh concerns about Beijing’s growing pressure on foreign correspondents.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.media:description: U.S. and Chinese flags flying over Tiananmen Square during President Trump’s visit to Beijing this month.

item.title: Vietnam’s Leader Warns Asia About the Risks of Superpower Conflict
item.description: In a speech to military leaders from the region, To Lam said that distrust and a lack of respect for established rules had created a culture of “the big fish swallowing the small fish.”
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: Vietnam’s leader, To Lam, in Singapore, giving the keynote address on Friday to an annual Asian security conference. While careful not to criticize the United States or China directly, Mr. Lam acknowledged that a series of threats is making it harder for his country and the region to prosper.

item.title: What’s Next for Blue Origin After Rocket Explosion
item.description: Jeff Bezos was gaining ground on Elon Musk’s SpaceX and Starlink. Thursday’s rocket explosion on a launchpad creates a major setback.
item.dc:creator: Karen Weise
item.media:description: Blue Origin’s New Glenn rocket exploded on its launchpad during a test.

item.title: A Hidden Treasure of Rare Snake Specimens
item.description: In the foothills of the Ecuadorean Amazon, a 101-year-old farmer and a young scientist turned an amateur collection into a scientific survey of one of the most diverse snake habitats on Earth.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil and Johanna Alarcón
item.media:description: Manuel Genaro Peñafiel Flores with some of his collection of snakes at home in Mera, Ecuador.

item.title: In ‘Fallen Angels,’ Kelli O’Hara and Rose Byrne Get Laughs Getting Sloshed
item.description: The Tony-nominated stars of “Fallen Angels” demonstrate how to act drunk onstage and have a hilarious hangover.
item.dc:creator: Jesse Green and Mohamed Sadek
item.media:credit: Mohamed Sadek for The New York Times

item.title: Is Spencer Pratt for Real?
item.description: The former reality star’s dark visions of Los Angeles are resonating in a heated mayoral race, even if they’re far from the truth.
item.dc:creator: Nathan Taylor Pemberton
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: “It’s not a kind of fame that is fun,” Spencer Pratt said of his political turn.

item.title: Uber to Offer Shuttles for Fans Leaving World Cup Matches
item.description: Soccer lovers frustrated by public transportation will have another option to return to Boston, Dallas, Miami and New York from hard-to-reach stadiums.
item.dc:creator: Christine Chung
item.media:description: Uber’s shared shuttles will take fans back to Manhattan from MetLife Stadium for $49.

item.title: Hungary Showed How to Defeat an Autocrat
item.description: Eleven lessons America must learn
item.dc:creator: M. Gessen and Máté Bartha
item.media:credit: Máté Bartha for The New York Times

item.title: Does Trump Want to Lose the Midterms?
item.description: The president doesn’t seem that concerned that his party could lose control of Congress. Ezra Klein and the Republican strategist Liam Donovan discuss Trump’s midterm strategy and Democratic paths to victory.
item.dc:creator: Ezra Klein and Jack McCordick

item.title: Strict Monitoring Could Delay Homecoming of Hantavirus Ship Passengers
item.description: Trump administration officials want local health authorities to constantly monitor the 18 passengers for another three weeks, a requirement that far exceeds typical protocols.
item.dc:creator: Jacey Fortin and Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: The passengers who have been housed in the National Quarantine Unit at the University of Nebraska Medical Center were aboard a cruise ship that became the center of a global hantavirus outbreak this month.

item.title: How NextEra Energy Wielded Political Power in Florida
item.description: NextEra, which is seeking to buy Dominion Energy, has often butted heads with consumer groups, residents and journalists in its home state.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.category: White House East Wing and Ballroom (Washington, DC)
item.media:description: A Dominion Energy power plant near Davis, W.Va. NextEra Energy’s political activities are expected to be scrutinized as it seeks approval of its purchase of Dominion.

item.title: Can New Technology Help Save North Atlantic Right Whales?
item.description: Innovative systems to keep ships from hitting North Atlantic right whales are coming into use. The Trump administration is weighing whether they can replace a bedrock protection.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times

item.title: Woman With No Right Hand Is Pulled Over for Using Phone in Her Right Hand
item.description: Her case was, of course, dismissed.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker
